comments.title: water as a source of souls
comments.content:
I have encountered many references over the years to the belief that souls to be born come from water sources to reach the womb. Either the sea or lakes. This belief is found across Eurasia, but can also be found in other places. There is a parallel belief that those souls of the recently dead have to go through or cross water to get to the otherworld. Often living people that take this journey also have to cross water, like Herakles, Odysseus, or Bran. This is a commonly known motif from mythology.

I see it as indicating the perception that water is liminal, a type of boundary, and connected to all life. We spend 9 months developing immersed in fluid. When I read about the Druid idea of souls developing and the stages involved, I thought there might be a connection. Is there some reason for this belief in occult or magical terms?

replies.title: Re: water as a source of souls
replies.content:
Traditional occult lore explains this very widespread tradition as a symbolic reflection of an important truth. Symbolically, in a great many myths and legends, the material plane (which includes the etheric realm) is represented by earth, the astral plane by water, the mental plane by air, and the spiritual planes by fire. So to say that souls come into incarnation via the water, and depart from incarnation the same way, is to express symbolically the idea that souls descend into matter and ascend from it again via the astral plane -- as, in occult teaching, they do.

This presupposes, of course, that the old mythologies have an inner meaning as well as their obvious one. That's a very widespread belief in ancient and modern times alike, though, and I'm inclined to think that it's correct.

comments.title: Egyptian Rebirth as Animals
comments.content:
Ancient Egyptians believed they would reincarnate as animals by default, something which by our current understanding is not true; humans reincarnate as humans.

Here is a speculation: I don't want to say the Egyptians were wrong, given their extensive magic technology; but I also don't want to say we are wrong. What if both are right... and something in their old practices for second death delaying caused this?

replies.title: Re: Egyptian Rebirth as Animals
replies.content: Okay, I'm a little confused here. First, what's your source for the idea that the ancient Egyptians believed that humans reincarnate as animals by default? I'm not familiar with that idea from the ancient Egyptian literature I've read; for example, that's not what The Book of the Dead says. Second, whose "current understanding" do you have in mind when you say that humans reincarnate as humans? In modern Druid teaching, for example, rebirth in an animal form is seen as a real possibility.

replies.title: Re: Egyptian Rebirth as Animals
replies.content:
I have a vague memory that mummification was done to avoid this; maybe my school texts were innacurate, and those were risks.

I said default; most humans will reincarnate as such for now (at least while there are bodies available; as decline kicks in, it won't be so).

replies.title: Re: Egyptian Rebirth as Animals
replies.content:
I can't speak to ancient Egyptian beliefs, but I do know that different teachings have different opinions about whether humans ever reincarnate as animals. The Michael Teaching says that this is possible, but it's a very unusual event to teach fairly extreme lessons as a corrective for going too far off the path. It's not something that happens as a matter of course, and it's not something that happens as frequently as, say, Hindu teachings would suggest.

IIRC, the example given was for someone who had spent lifetimes hunting animals for sport might spend a lifetime as a fox being hunted to get that side of the experience.

John Roth

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: On-line Tarot Yes or No readings.
replies.content:
I don't see why it wouldn't be. While the pseudorandom number generation process is itself deterministic, it's generally seeded from the computer's clock. That doesn't strike as any different in principle from how horary astrology works.

That said, I wouldn't consider Astrology.com a reliable source for any kind of divination. They seem to be of the "just write anything; all this stuff's nonsense anyway" school of horoscopes, and I suspect their Tarot interpretations are of the same quality.

replies.title: Re: On-line Tarot Yes or No readings.
replies.content:
I've gotten generally accurate geomancy results using the =RAND() function on Microsoft Excel, so I think there's something to this. Since the seed is essentially random (as even if you know the underlying deterministic tables/algorithms, you don't know at which exact millisecond you'll hit the "generate" button), so is the reading.

The difference between truly random numbers and pseudorandom numbers mostly becomes an issue if you are generating a huge quantity of random numbers at the same time - well above the number you'd be generating for a single divinatory reading.

Beim Himmel über mir,
bei der Erde unter mir,
beim Feuer zu meiner Rechten,
beim Wasser zu meiner Linken:
Mögen die Mächte der Natur, mich und die Meinen segnen und beschützen, jetzt und für immer.

Beim Maifalken im Morgenwind rufe ich die Luft, ihre Götter, ihre Geister und ihre Kräfte.
Ich bitte die Luft um ihren Segen.
Ich danke der Luft für ihre Gaben.
Und mit Hilfe der Luft banne ich in und um mir alle ihre unbalancierten Erscheinungsformen.

comments.title: Will v. algorithm
comments.content:
A friend sent me a link to an article repeating the too oft told story of a somewhat directionless single white male turning to a popular video streaming service and getting sucked down an alt-right rabbit hole. This particular individual realized what had happened, changed political agendas, and is participating in a left-leaning counter action. I do not wish to discuss politics either way but instead want to make an observation and ask a question.

Reading the article it stuck me that what we are seeing is a change in consciousness brought about through the act -- not of a will -- but of an algorithm. In this case it is the algorithm that suggests the next video to watch. Now, yes, there are very human and all-too-profit oriented wills behind the programming of the algorithm but its action on an individual viewer takes place in private without public review.

The article also discussed the allure that the subject felt and the empowerment he drew from watching strongly opinionated material trending all the way through to hate speech. Again I do not want to discuss the specific material but admit the lure that knowledge has, particularly when presented as "occult." There is seduction in feelings of membership, shared culture, secret knowledge that the subject felt and that drew him further down into nastier material.

As a long time reader I doubt I have to explain this to you. Instead I would like to ask about the will versus algorithm distinction. Will is what I least understand in your (Dion Fortune's) definition of magic. What does the intermediation of an algorithm mean? You have always cautioned about the power of magic; I particularly like the jam sandwich analogy you wrote years ago. I have this image of the Silicon Valley "masters of technology" dripping in raspberry jam from the unsupervised actions of the algorithms they turn loose in pursuit of profit.

I also ask how traditional occult systems handle its seductive nature and the exploitation to which that can lead. Is it prevented through the teacher/student relationship? A good Zen teacher, for instance, is meant to offer continual and individual review of a student and would be immediately aware if one was "going astray." Is it that feedback cycle that prevents radicalization? Algorithms run at scale and are monitored, but their individual human effects go unsupervised. Is it this lack of feedback that leads to trouble? Are there lessons to be learned from solo occultists getting into trouble? (Beyond Mickey Mouse doing the dishes, of course!)

There is much more I would like to ask, but do not want to monopolize this channel. Please indicate if there is a more appropriate venue (Ecosophia comments?) perhaps one giving you an editorial input before sharing.

replies.title: Re: Will v. algorithm
replies.content:
May I ask, have you considered that the algorithm may be subject to will, either from alt-right mages, who have an obvious stake in the matter, or possibly from spiritual beings that range all the way the demonic to divine?

An algorithm directs the flow of information, but it seems to me that they would have many points in which the will of other beings could act on them. Certainly the whole "Elsagate" fiasco got me thinking along these lines, as there seemed to me to be a rather obvious sinister consciousness directing things toward specific nefarious ends, rather than just some computer code. Just because humans create something doesn't mean that they are in control of their creation.

replies.title: Re: Will v. algorithm
replies.content:
One remark... the algorithms you are talking about are probabilistic. That means they are not 100% determined but have a random element (which by the way also means they are no algorithms in the strict sense of the word). As far as I understand it, random events are the (only?) porthole through which magic and divination work out in the material world.

I wonder if that makes them hugely vulnerable against accidental or deliberate magical attacks....

Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Will v. algorithm
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, keep in mind that the entire narrative of that "too oft told story" has an ulterior motive. Having defined the alt-Right as evil incarnate, the cultural mainstream has to come up with a narrative to explain why it's become such a significant cultural factor over the last few years, without mentioning any of the actual issues involved. (You might find this discussion of the issues in question helpful.) The sort of thinking that treats people who get involved in the alt-Right as passive victims of an algorithm is very useful as a way to duck the fact that a great many people these days are rejecting the officially approved narratives for solid, specific, and very often economic reasons.

That said, an algorithm that includes randomizing or pseudo-randomizing factors -- as I believe all of them do -- is a happy hunting ground for magical influence, on the part of human beings and of beings other than human. Anything where small chance variations can work big changes is tailor made for mages and spirits to influence...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Being a music teacher and having sung in many different choirs I can attest to having sung all sorts of things I don't believe in as others imagine I do (including the dreaded Itsy Bitsy Spider, with a smile even.) I believe in the music, its power to transform people's lives, the rhythms and melodies and dances, and sometimes the lyrics.

When I sang Hindu and Muslim sacred songs in Indonesia, I was deeply moved by their beauty and the beauty of their imagery, and I participated fully in the religious rituals (It's a really bad idea to offend the local gods when you're a mere visitor in their realm.) Singing hymns to Ganesha and Vishnu in college taught me a lot about Indian culture and certainly piqued my interest in polytheism, but it didn't convert me.

Singing in Episcopal, Pentecostal, and black Baptist choirs has given me a window into how wildly diverse Christianity's god actually is. So when some unfortunate song drones on incessantly about choosing Jesus as my personal savior, I sing quite sincerely about how important it is that we each DO get to choose which gods we will dedicate ourselves to. Songs about Christ and the holy spirit are much easier to find meaning in since Christ consciousness is, like Buddha nature, what we all seek - the god within. The holy spirit descends upon Barong dancers taking them into trance, Voudon practitioners being ridden by Oshun, and Pentecostals speaking in tongues.

If you love the music, sing it with joy! Find the meaning that works for you. Everyone else in the choir is singing to a slightly different god based on their own unique understanding. Anyone who tells you they know exactly who the god is and exactly how you have to believe to please him has claimed a power reserved for the gods, not mere mortals. Yes, you will find new depth and guidance in the myths and images of the gods you sing to - Ganesha will now always be my go-to for the removal of obstacles. Is that expanded pantheon a bad thing?

If you don't tell people that you see Jesus as a purple river god riding on a crocodile, they're very unlikely to ask. They'll just be overjoyed that you're singing to the same god they are - their fire-eyed, brimstone-hurling, table-turning scourge. It's probably a good thing that we have no way of knowing to whom our choir mates are really singing.

comments.title: Deified dead and pomo magic
comments.content:
1) If an exceptionally accomplished dead person is deified (Chinese mythology for one is full of them), or at least frequently thought of with reverence by many people over a long time (Alexander the Great comes to mind), what kind of effect that has on their soul? Does this create a new entity that can be called upon, or perhaps alter an existing deity? If it does something, how does this fit together with rebirth? The existence of ancestor worship hints of some part of human soul staying out there while the rest passes on, but I don't really know.

2) Not a question, but a thought on postmodern magic. It seems that even if different energies/deities/whatever were nothing but human-created symbols, it's still a bad idea to think you can make them mean whatever you want. The human mind is a much larger thing than its everyday consciousness, and a single mind is nothing compared to the weight of centuries embodied within the culture. In magic, you want as much of your mind as possible to focus on precisely the same thing, and that's not as simple as one might think.

Assume a witch does a working that uses Mars as a symbol of proud feminine and compassionate inclusivity. Her everyday self uses it so, but in what's usually called subconscious, it's going to conflict with culture-defined ideas of what Mars symbolizes, linguistic connections like Mars/martial, the link between color red and aggression, impressions from past lives etc. If her concentration is strong, the working might fizzle out as its contradictions fight it out in her head, but more likely than not, it will just veer off in a direction she didn't expect. Less obviously, by assuming that the elements of working can mean whatever you like, you sort of give the different parts of your subconscious a permission to freely interpret them too, and since this leads to lots of different interpretations of working's intent and the employed symbolism, the unity of focus will suffer. By contrast, if you work with an established system that you believe cannot be changed willy-nilly, as it relies on objective facts, your sub-minds are more likely to stay on the same page.

Comments? I know that for you this is just a bunch of words leading to a place from which one should start anyway, since the things we invoke mostly aren't our creations, but if this was more hit than miss, perhaps the argument is of some occasional utility.

I recently found a deel (a traditional Mongolian robe) for sale in Inner Mongolia that was covered in interlinked swastikas.

So, it's not just India. It's all through China, Japan, Central Asia, and South-East Asia as well. That's a lot of people.In fact, it might not be too far off the mark to say that the bulk of humanity view the swastika as a postive symbol.

replies.title: Re: Deified dead and pomo magic
replies.content:
1) In many cases, an exceptionally accomplished person is in his or her last human life, and won't have to worry about rebirth anyway -- in Druid teachings, the state we enter when we finish learning the lessons of human incarnation (and material incarnation in general) is one that no longer requires the loss of memory that comes with rebirth. Souls in that condition are able to respond to requests for help from incarnate humans, and the more people invoke them, the more energy they have to answer those requests. That's where saints, Chinese gods, and a great many other holy beings come from.

2) That strikes me as a good logical way to make sense of the phenomena from within a psychological interpretation of magic -- and since one of the foremost 20th century occultists, Carl Jung, worked successfully with such an interpretation, I'm not going to quibble with it anything like as much as you seem to expect. ;-)

in my personal experience the more spiritual work the more friends have drifted away. In fact I've lost nearly all of my friends since taking up practice.

Why is this? Well one aspect is that this is "the way of the lonely ones," viz. spiritual practice marks one as an outsider.

This may be especially true now given the prevalent bad vibes. As your aura brightens it will scare away a lot of people. As you related last week, not everyone who is in your circles comes with good intent and banishing rituals do a very good job of chasing those sorts of people away. This has many practical benefits, of course, but as your energy shifts with time spent in practice this will inevitably change you in ways that people find acutely discomfiting. By repeatedly invoking a deity that means, ipso facto, that you are in a relationship with that deity, and one in which you will change and become more infused with the energies of that deity.

This is important and sacred work, but unless people are "on the bus," there will likely be a tendency for losing touch and contact with folks who, no matter how kind, sincere and big-hearted, simply aren't on a compatible path.

You may also come to reevaluate relationships from the new bright light that has come into your life and grow to feel differently. Certainly I found that to be the case after about a year of practice.

For whatever it's worth, you certainly have my encouragement and I'm delighted to hear that you are getting such good results so rapidly. That said, TSW indeed, and sometimes in ways that can be extremely painful.

Allen Hynek was a student of Rudolf Steiner's teachings in his younger days, and never accepted the "hardware hypothesis" that UFOs were spacecraft from another world. comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Thanks as always for Magic Mondays.

I've read here several times the jar method of breaking ties with people in your life, and I was wondering if there's a similar method to help break ties with a place. The property I live on is carved out of what once was my grandparent's farm -- an idyllic place to grow up on -- but the land around our two acres was divided up and sold off after my grandmother's passing in 2001. Ever since, I have walked the boundaries, either along our property lines or on the roads (it's inescapable) with a great amount of loss and bitterness in my heart. It has all slowly changed under new ownership -- two new family homes, and just this spring a new owner began tearing up the back field where we used to fly kites and pick berries. I trespassed to "check things out" a week ago, and I was glad I had taken photos and video last year, because it's not going to remain the back field much longer.

It seems a terrible weight to carry for so long, and I regularly have nightmares about going back on the land and discovering shopping malls or various other intrusions on the overwhelmingly natural world I grew up in. Thankfully, due to local zoning laws, it will only ever be houses, but it's still development, always right in front of my eyes, always out of my control.

This area has undergone a lot of development in the past decades, and I always hate to see the trees coming down for more houses, but it's so much more personal with my grandparent's land because I was so intimately involved with since the moment I could sit in the grass. From planting and harvesting the massive vegetable garden, to my grandfather's "standing stones", to my favorite trees, to spending hours at the side of the pond or in the woods -- I feel like there's still a ghost of me on that land, and I'd like to get her off of it, move her into a future separate from what was and what is now beyond her control. My mother and I cherish our two acres and the home we have, and I have been putting more effort into appreciating that, but I still have the nightmares, and the feelings of dread, bitterness, and sorrow every time I pass the opening to the back field.

Curiously, on the lot right beside us is the house I lived in until I was 7, and I have a great fondness for that property but none of the grief. Granted, it hasn't changed much under its various new owners, but I have no sense of "wanting it back" as I do with my grandparent's land.

I've worked with this for so long now, I'm hoping there is some other way to let it go than by simply trying to. I always keep in mind that the trees will take it all back in the end no matter what, and that's some solace, but I still feel that personal connection, almost a responsibility of guardianship, that I'm legally prohibited from enacting.

I'm sure many others who have lived "out in the country" have experienced similar losses in these times, and I'd be open to hearing any ways of working with it, as well as any insights or perspectives that might move me forward. I'd just like to live without this constant sense of loss, and moving away isn't an option, at least not as long as my mother is still here.

Thanks as always,
Diane

I also thought of my grandfather, who passed away first in 1995, who was sent home from the Army in WWII after a nervous breakdown and retired early to spend the rest of his life just taking care of the land. He never talked about it, but now and then I'd come across a tractor seat, neatly painted red or yellow, nailed to a tree stump with a particularly nice view out before it. He always said never to sell the land, but none of his children wanted to keep it. My grandmother called it "God's Country". Both were originally city people and just found so much joy and solace out in nature, and I wonder if some of the disappointment and grief hanging about it is also theirs.

There was also a very old beech tree, somewhat in the center of it all, with a great span of winding branches, so big and broad I used to chant to it when I would pass beneath it, hoping it would never fall on me. One winter, after the land was sold, all the limbs came down around the old hollow trunk in an ice storm. I took a three-foot chunk of it home, and maybe now I'm supposed to make something of it, and place it where it can continue to keep watch from the safety of our acreage.

I'll certainly do some further journaling and meditating, but thank you for turning me away from breaking ties and towards listening and tuning in something else, perhaps, that needs to be done. It is good to have the reminder sometimes that things are often bigger than perhaps we want them to be.

comments.title: The Magical Garden
comments.content:
Question for JMG and gardeners in general;

Let's say that you are in a real grim place with bad vibes galore. You are walking down the sidewalk and come upon a community garden that happens to be planted with hawthorn, holly, cedar, rosemary, hyssop, angelica, and every other protective and blessing herb you can think of. You speak to a kindly gardener and she says that the garden has been established for some decades

My question is, basically, in this thought experiment, how far would you expect the garden's good energies to extend? A block? Five? As far away as you could smell the aromatics? Only within the precincts of the garden itself?

A city like Portland Oregon show demonstratively that really bad vibes can be balanced with good enough greenery. My curiosity it how far this extends, how powerful it is, and how much it can balance on its own. Forgive the absurdity of the framing, but is it that a one acre space planted in protective plants balance out 7 skid row alcoholics or 2 professional level malefic magical practitioners? I'm aware it's difficult to quantify this, but it seems worthy to broach the question to get a sense of how effective the magical garden is over space.

replies.title: Re: The Magical Garden
replies.content:
Thank you for that great question! Can't really speak about the magical side, but I am asking myself similar question in a more "worldly" framing frequently. Maybe my observations and thoughts can shed some light on the magical side as well?

We have taken over a small patch of land in a rural village some years ago. "clean gardens" everywhere. Only a mowed lawn is a good lawn. Fallen leaves are considered to be dirt. And so on. We stopped mowing the lawn except once a year at the end of summer. We planted some fruit trees alongside several wild trees and bushes. We turned a small part of the land into a vegetable garden, where we also keep a few beehives. Insect population has since risen significantly, also the diversity of plant and insect species. Slowly, the land (it's really a rather small patch, well below 5000 square metres) seems to become more and more alive and enchanted, as far as I can tell. Does this aliveness spread? Difficult to say. But what does spread, I feel, is the idea of this aliveness and what it means. At first, we got a lot of raised eyebrows and also experienced some setbacks. But as the garden becomes more alive and the harvest becomes larger every year, people around us begin to notice. Also, when you talk to others, you can do so differently and more heartfelt, because of this experience. I feel that this really makes a difference.

To come back to your question... I think that the setting has a big influence on the matter. As well as the power you put into that piece of land and the powers that are already there and those that you manage to evoke. How strong is the counterpressure of your surroundings? I'd say if you "press" harder than the surroundings press back, your work will spread. If there is little or no counterpressure at all, you will run into open doors. If the pressure of the surrounding is overwhelming, it will be the opposite.

Cheers,
Nachtgurke

comments.title: Esoteric Value of Secrecy

replies.title: Re: Esoteric Value of Secrecy
replies.content:
First, it teaches you to pay attention to your habits of unthinking speech. You can't just drift through life in the vague half-doze that counts as "being awake" for most people -- you actually have to pay attention to what you're saying and whom you're saying it to, and that all by itself can jolt you into a higher state of consciousness.

Second, it helps build an inner life that's separate from the collective consciousness of your time. To have a secret and keep it is to be isolated from others, to know something they don't, to stand apart from the collective mind. Chosen freely, the practice of secrecy makes you an individual, not simply one more cog in the machine.

replies.title: Re: Homeopathy and magic
replies.content:
I've studied and practiced (mostly) classical homeopathy for over a decade so would say that most of us would call that an "aggravation" and attribute it to one or more of the following: a remedy that was not quite similar enough, too high a potency, or repeated too many times. That said, it's a common occurrence and usually has no lasting ill effects. Each remedy has unique spheres of correspondences as well as typical duration of action. As someone who has a lot of fire in their natal chart I've experienced (and caused) a number of these aggravations. In the long run I tend to view it as somewhat analogous to the hygiene hypothesis regarding germ exposure--crudely paraphrased, "that which doesn't kill you.."

Anyway, a classic hpath tries to avoid causing them because the goal is a gentle, seamless re-tunement of the vital force (etheric +/- astral +/- mental? bodies) but often after even a severe aggravation there will be a deep progressive improvement in the overall health. Per Hering, the true simillimum cures from the inside out (last stop is the skin), head downward, and in reverse chronology, oldest to most recent symptoms. It's generally wise to wait until everything seems at the new normal before taking another remedy.

I would concur that being more in tune to your subtle bodies due to magical practice would likely make you more sensitive to a subtle modality such as homeopathy. Healthy people can tolerate higher potencies much more safely (yes, it is possible to cause harm with very high potencies such as M and above) Some rare sensitives can access the healing effects just by hearing the name of the remedy. (Cats are also extremely sensitive to them :)

I think it's this "magical" nature of homeopathic remedies that singles homeopathy out for more hate by the conventional school (my origins) even while other so-called energy medicine modalities functioning in the same subtle realms are tolerated and even covered by insurance.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I'm trying to understand what you're saying here, and not getting far. How on earth do you get the idea that questions in the 11th and 3rd houses always show perfection? They don't automatically get occupation, conjunction, mutation or translation, and the only "automatic" aspect is the sextile, which doesn't count because you don't read aspects between the houses of the querent and the quesited.

It occurs to me that you may mean the 12th and 2nd houses, on the assumption that the figures in them are always next to the figure in the first. That doesn't count as conjunction; to make a conjunction, one of the figures has to pass elsewhere in the chart and enter into conjunction; so the figure in the 12th has to also appear in the 2nd, and vice versa, for it to count as a conjunction. If it doesn't pass, it doesn't count.

2) I'd read RW Conjunctio, LW Via, Judge Carcer as "you can keep it if you want, but you won't learn anything about it and will end up letting go of it."

You've mentioned "alt-right" mages making use of chaos magic a few times.

I just wonder if you have any experience or knowledge of using traditional magic to manipulate electronic media. As far as I know, basically a traditional mage would invoke the spirits for his specific end the way he and his predecessors had without specifying the medium, electronic or otherwise. But is there anything someone invoking traditional magical powers could do to form a "digital" link? For example, creating a talisman of Venus hosted on a webpage to draw in customers to a jewellery e-commerce store.

replies.title: Re: Traditional magic and electronic media
replies.content: It's not something I've looked into, but it ought to work -- and yes, the most effective approach is to focus on goals and let the means take care of themselves.

comments.content:
I've got a theory and I'd like to know if you can think of anything to test it, or potential avenues to look for further information about it. Autism seems to be linked to some rather unusual damage to the central nervous system, and seems to have seen a spike in recent years. I work with children on a regular basis and it seems plausible to me the rates of autism have increased. Despite the many neurological issues, many of us seem to be precocious in many ways, and this leads me to an interesting thought: Is it possible autism is the result of brain damage caused by a soul trying to express adult capabilities in a child's body?

This would explain the odd partial precocious nature of many autistics, and it would also explain why in many cases it takes a while to show up: the brain damage is gradual, and it doesn't happen all at once but over time.

Finally, it would also provide an explanation for the increase in the rate of autism, as souls are spending much less time out of incarnation than normal, and so are more likely to try to express capabilities they had in the last life time. Do you know of any lore that might help me investigate this further?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That seems very plausible to me. I'm not really sure how to pursue it further, though books discussing the afterlife from an occult perspective might be one way to begin that -- Dion Fortune's Through the Gates of Death is one readily available example.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you. I'll have to look into it.

Two ways to test it spring to mind: the first is to look at gaps between last lifetimes with autistics, who should display much shorter intervals; and if this is the case, autistics should also have much higher rates of various forms of dysphoria, such as gender dysphoria.

Another bit of support springs to mind: according to some studies I've looked at, it's quite common for autistics to be breathtakingly precocious babies, speaking, walking, and moving much earlier than usual, and then run into issues between about 1-2 years of age.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I was emotionally precocious. At the age of just a few months, I would start crying from grief -- not your ordinary baby-wailing, but showing every sign of profound sadness -- when certain specific songs were sung in my hearing. They were songs that were popular during my previous lifetime -- so that would support your thesis.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Much higher dysphoria rates should check out; IIRC the ratsphere* has commented about studies showing exactly that, plus run some internal surveys of their own with the same result.

(And after some nosing through a particular old SSC post I found one of the old relevant articles: https://www.theatlantic.com/health/archive/2016/11/the-link-between-autism-and-trans-identity/507509/. The SSC article that linked to it [https://slatestarcodex.com/2017/06/28/why-are-transgender-people-immune-to-optical-illusions/] might also be worth taking a look at, if you don't mind that article's firmly materialist viewpoint.)

I'm probably an anecdote in favor of a short gap between lifetimes with people on the spectrum, but I'm going to be an extremely unusual case: my last incarnation died effectively spending his lifeforce to cross some sort of dimensional/parallel/spacetime gate (reminiscent of descriptions of the sipapu of Hopi myth, though square and horizontal) and incarnated immediately afterwards.

(Additional anecdote: "[pretentious_username] had his teenage rebellion when he was six" is one of my family's favorite ha-ha-only-serious jokes.)


* - superset of the LessWrong types; fairly nifty circle once you get away from the hardline materialists towards the postrationalist types, and I continue to suspect some subset of them are going to synthesize the Faustian equivalent of Neoplatonism at some point.

- pretentious_username

comments.content:
Hi JMG,

First and foremost, all my thanks for hosting this. I don't usually make it back to reply and thank you, so I hope the thanks in advance is satisfactory. I always look forward to these discussions.

I have a couple questions for you. I've heard you mention before that stones/oils/etc have their own properties and energies, and I would like to know more about this. I'm wondering if you or any of the forum know of any reliable books or websites with this sort of information? There is such an abundance of information on the internet and most of it trying to sell you something, that I don't know who to trust.

2. About a year ago my partner and I had an incident where I hurt him greatly without meaning to. On the surface we've resolved the issue and intellectually we've forgiven each other, however beneath that I think there's still a lot of hurt and anger that I'm not sure how to fix. This leads to us having less patience with each other in general, and fights that spiral out of control far quicker than they should. Obviously we'll resolve this through time and me showing how much I still care for him, but I was wondering if it would be worthwhile to do any sort of natural magic here, I'm thinking maybe something to help us both remember to have patience with each other and help cleanse the lingering resentment between us. I don't have any experience with magic at all so natural magic would be the only thing I could work with. I'm curious to try some things and was wondering if this might be an appropriate instance?

Thanks again for all you do,
Cym

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Well, I wrote this book titled The Encyclopedia of Natural Magic which is about exactly that subject, and it might be worth a look. ;-)

2) Do you practice any form of divination -- tarot cards, or anything like that? When considering a working like this, the first thing to do is check with the universe to make sure it's a good idea, and divination's the standard way to do that. If you get a favorable response, talk to your partner and get his permission; better still, involve him in the process of choosing what to do and how to do it. The results will be much better if both of you are willing it to happen.

comments.title: My mother
comments.content:
I've been trying take some distance from my parents the last few months, asking them to respect my desire for distance but my mother seems unable to.

She finally texted me this after I asked yet again to "Please respect my desire for distance":

"I'm around that I will keep reaching out. Someday I will catch you in a moment of weakness and you will find your way back to Dad and me. In the meantime, be well Mom"

..What does that say to you?

replies.title: Re: My mother
replies.content:
"I'm afraid that I will keep reaching out."

Not 'around'

replies.title: Re: My mother
replies.content:
What that says to me is that your mother is a manipulative creep. You have to decide what you're going to do in response. Can you block her number? Are you willing to move and not give your parents your new contact info?

Full disclosure: I've had no contact with my mother for more than a quarter century, and having her out of my life has been a blessing. Sometimes that's the only thing you can do.

replies.title: Re: My mother
replies.content:
Can't thank you enough for responding.

I needed an outsider's perspective without the internal bias.

Right now they pay for my car insurance and a few other things ...and have used finances over me my entire life. I will finish my BA (finally) at the end of December and I will finally be able to move out of state with the prospect of actually earning a livable wage.

It's been a long, long limbo. I'd do anything to stand on my own two feet far, far away from them.

replies.title: Re: My mother
replies.content: See if you can get at least one good-sized river or an arm of the sea between where they live and where you'll be living -- that will sharply limit the effect of certain energetic connections. Regular practice of a good banishing ritual such as the Sphere of Protection will also help -- in your place I'd start doing that now, to weaken the connection.

There's a site, tvtropes.com, that seems to suck people in. Once someone has a tab on it, they tend to open tab after tab, and get sucked in for hours, looking at pointless things, and strangely things they agree are pointless while looking at them. I avoid going there because it's happened to me before, and so the site strikes me as dangerous. Is there something in the occult lore which may explain this?

XKCD seems to have nailed it with one comic*, which says this is a major issue for more people than just the handful I know have this issue. It just seems very odd that a site would be able to suck people in for hours on end, so is there anything in occult lore which may help explain it?

*https://xkcd.com/609/

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Interesting. No, I don't know of anything in particular. One useful trick, though -- have a good adblocker and don't whitelist tvtropes.com. That will limit you to 10 pages a month.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't store cookies or history when I close my browser, so that doesn't work, but I've found an alternative: if I ever find a tvtropes tab open, I shut down my computer and go for a walk. Gets me exercise, fresh air, and solves the problem, so I consider it a win all around.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Excellent. Now meditate on that until you understand it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
(Not the OP)

... Because the subtle bodies need to learn to interact with the world, the same way the physical body does, and because the world isn't just the good (for humans) bits of the world?

I've given this kind of thing some thought before -- I have two young children (and a partner with autoimmune issues she thinks come partly from extreme underexposure to dirt as a child) and from that perspective I -- as a rank amateur -- might draw a parallel between banishing rituals covering the infant's home and, say, obsessively clearing nettles and applying insect repellent before letting them play in the garden, or constantly disinfecting the house so they never encounter germs. Similarly I could see the need to avoid serious magical work around small children as the equivalent of gently keeping them away from slug pellets, dogs, dubious strangers, etc. But just because I can see it doesn't mean it's what you're getting at, of course!
